nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reefs and lagoons on sandy or gravelly substrates and around live coral, generally cryptic among rubble and rockwork. Most often observed at night when it emerges from cover to forage.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with abundant small crevices and overhangs
rubble and gravel patches for foraging
shaded nano-reef setup for nocturnal viewing
stable mature reef with established microfauna
covered intakes and tight-fitting lid (general invertebrate precaution)

diet.notes: Has been documented using the anemones held in its claws as a feeding apparatus, mopping food particles and detritus from rock and substrate and then transferring them to the mouthparts with the maxillipeds. In captivity readily accepts small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, finely chopped marine fish or shellfish, brine shrimp, and copepods, supplemented occasionally with dried algae sheets (e.g. nori or spirulina). Target-feeding near the crab's shelter tends to work better than broadcast feeding, as it is shy and slow-moving.

goodWith: small peaceful reef shrimp (sexy shrimp, peppermint, bumblebee)
cleaner shrimp and porcelain anemone crabs
small reef snails and microfauna cleanup crews
soft corals, LPS, and zoanthids (in well-fed tanks)
small peaceful reef fish (clown gobies, firefish, dartfish) in larger tanks
avoidWith: predatory crustaceans such as coral banded shrimp and large pistol shrimp
any fish large enough to swallow a ~2 cm crab

summary: The Pom Pom Crab (Lybia tessellata) is a small Indo-Pacific reef crab, reaching about 2.5 cm, famous for carrying a tiny living anemone in each claw — typically Triactis producta or a Bunodeopsis species — and waving them defensively. Nocturnal and generally peaceful, it suits mature nano reef aquaria. Not to be confused with the freshwater Pom Pom Crab, Ptychognathus barbatus.
careNotes: The Pom Pom Crab (also widely sold as the Boxer Crab) is one of the most popular nano-reef oddballs because of its symbiosis with the anemones it carries in its chelae. The anemones — most often documented as Triactis producta or Bunodeopsis species — are used both for defence (the crab waves and prods at perceived threats with them) and as a feeding tool (the anemones' tentacles catch detritus and particles that the crab then transfers to its mouth). If a crab loses an anemone it will frequently split a remaining one in two; specimens are often imported holding nothing, in which case they generally tolerate captivity well and may pick up small anemones or coral fragments over time. Tank size guidance across sources ranges from around 20 L (5 US gal) up to around 76 L (20 US gal); a floor of 40 L is a reasonable target, as the added water volume provides better parameter stability for a long-lived specimen. Like all marine invertebrates the species is highly copper-sensitive — never use copper-based medications in a tank housing this crab. It is shy and largely nocturnal, so a tank with abundant overhangs and a 'lights-off' acclimation is generally advised when introducing a new specimen.
breedingNotes: Females have been reported to carry distinctive red eggs brooded on the abdomen, and spawning in well-kept reef tanks is not unusual. Successful captive rearing is considered difficult: the larvae are planktonic and require specialised kreisel-style larval-rearing setups with frequent feedings of live microfoods (rotifers, enriched nauplii, microalgae). No widely-published hobbyist protocol exists, and larvae are typically lost to filtration or predation within days in a standard reef tank.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, native range (Indo-Pacific, Hawaii), tank size (~10 US gal), temperament, and reef-safe status. Confirmed the species carries Bunodeopsis anemones used to gather food from the water column. Recommended introduction with lights off.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder
additional.notes: Third cross-check source. Confirmed family Xanthidae, adult size ~1 inch, lifespan 2–5 years, water parameters (25–28°C / SG 1.023–1.025 / pH 8.1–8.4 / dKH 8–12), reef safety, copper sensitivity, omnivore diet, and difficult larval breeding.

additional.name: FishKeeper UK
additional.notes: Fourth cross-check source. Confirmed max size ~1 inch, water parameters (23–27°C / SG 1.020–1.025 / pH 8.1–8.4), peaceful temperament, reef-safe, copper intolerance, and nano-reef stocking guidance.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Lybia tessellata)
additional.notes: Taxonomic / natural-history reference. Confirmed family Xanthidae, order Decapoda, distribution (Indo-Pacific from Red Sea/East Africa to Hawaii and northern Japan), anemone species (Triactis producta and Bunodeopsis spp.), and that the crab cannot feed without the anemones — it uses their tentacles to collect particles and removes them with its maxillipeds.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Healthy adults are reported to moult roughly every 3–6 weeks; juveniles moult more frequently. Newly moulted specimens are vulnerable for a day or two and should have shelter; shed exoskeletons can be left in the tank as a calcium source. Sources note that the anemones are sometimes temporarily set down during a moult and re-collected afterwards.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Females routinely carry red egg masses in captivity, but planktonic larvae require dedicated rearing setups with live microfeeds and are rarely raised outside of specialist hands.
